content:
Everyone's building armies of AI "specialists" inside Claude Code. Most of them never trigger, collide with each other, and quietly bloat the very context window they were supposed to protect. I built and stress-tested 100 subagents — official built-ins, the big community collections, and a pile of my own — to find the handful that genuinely earn their keep. Here are the 12 I actually delegate to, the ones I deleted, and the uncomfortable truth about what a subagent is really for.

Why I Went Down This Rabbit Hole

This is the third time I've done this to myself. First it was 100 Claude Skills. Then 100 MCP servers. Now: subagents. Together they're the three pillars of the Claude Code stack — Skills give an agent competence, MCP servers give it capability, and subagents give it delegation. I'd covered two. The trilogy demanded the third.

And subagents are where the hype is loudest right now. Open GitHub and you'll find collections with hundreds of them: VoltAgent's awesome-claude-code-subagents ships 154+ agents across 10 categories with 22.9k stars; wshobson's marketplace packs 194 agents, 158 skills, and 16 orchestrators into 37.5k stars. The pitch is intoxicating: assemble a team of AI specialists — a security-auditor, a react-specialist, a kubernetes-specialist, a quant-analyst — and let Claude Code dispatch the right expert for every task.

So I did the obvious thing. I installed, wired up, and actually used 100 subagents across real work: code review, debugging, test runs, security audits, database analysis, incident triage. I watched which ones Claude actually delegated to, which ones sat inert, and which ones quietly made my main conversation worse.

Most got deleted. Not because they were badly written — many were excellent — but because I'd fundamentally misunderstood what a subagent is for. That misunderstanding is the whole point of this article, and I'll get to it before the list.

This is the shortlist that survived. Twelve subagents. Out of a hundred.

TL;DR

•
A subagent is not a personality. It's a context firewall. Each one runs in its own isolated context window and returns only a summary to your main thread. That isolation — not the "expert persona" — is the actual product.

•
More subagents is worse, not better. Overlapping description fields make Claude delegate to the wrong one (or none). A zoo of 100 agents triggers less reliably than a sharp set of 10.

•
The three real jobs of a subagent: (1) isolate verbose output, (2) enforce tool/permission restrictions, (3) specialize behavior — optionally with persistent memory.

•
Model routing is a cost lever. Route cheap tasks to Haiku, deep reasoning to Opus (or Fable for the longest-horizon work). A well-configured fleet is dramatically cheaper than running everything on your main model.

•
My 12 keepers below cover review, debugging, testing, security, architecture, performance, data, docs, and orchestration — the spine of real engineering work.

•
This completes the trilogy: MCP = capability, Skills = competence, Subagents = delegation. The meta-skill across all three is the same: curate ruthlessly.

A 30-Second Refresher: What Is a Subagent?

A subagent is a specialized assistant that Claude Code can hand a task to. Mechanically, it's a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter:

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Expert code review specialist. Use immediately after writing or modifying code.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
---
You are a senior code reviewer ensuring high standards of code quality and security.
When invoked:
1. Run git diff to see recent changes
2. Focus on modified files
3. Begin review immediately
[checklist and output format...]

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Four things make that little file powerful, and they map exactly to the three jobs above:

•
Its own context window. This is the headline. The subagent does not see your conversation history, the files you've already read, or the skills you've already loaded. It starts fresh, does its work in isolation, and returns only a summary. Your main context stays clean.

•
Scoped tools. The tools field is an allowlist (disallowedTools is the denylist). A reviewer gets Read, Grep, Glob and physically cannot edit files. That's a security boundary, not a suggestion.

•
Its own model. The model field routes the subagent to sonnet, opus, haiku, fable, a full model ID, or inherit. This is your cost dial.

•
A focused system prompt. The Markdown body becomes the subagent's entire system prompt — not appended to Claude Code's default, but the prompt. Narrow expertise, no distraction.

Where they live

Location
Scope
Priority

Managed settings
Organization-wide
Highest

--agents CLI flag
Current session
High

.claude/agents/
Current project (check into git!)
Medium

~/.claude/agents/
All your projects
Low

Plugin agents/ directory
Where the plugin is enabled
Lowest

Project subagents belong in version control so your whole team delegates to the same reviewer. And you don't hand-write them from scratch — you ask Claude Code to write one for you (as of recent versions, /agents just reminds you to do exactly that), then refine the frontmatter.

The built-ins you already have

Before you install anything, Claude Code ships with subagents working on your behalf:

•
Explore — a fast, read-only agent for searching and understanding a codebase. It deliberately skips your CLAUDE.md and git status to stay cheap, and keeps all that search output out of your main context.

•
Plan — a read-only research agent used in plan mode to gather context before proposing a plan.

•
general-purpose — the do-everything agent for complex, multi-step tasks needing both exploration and action.

If you've used plan mode or watched Claude "explore the codebase," you've already been using subagents. That's the tell for what they're really about.

A Detour: The Uncomfortable Truth About Subagents

Here's the misunderstanding I mentioned. Most people — including past me — think of a subagent as a character: a little AI expert with a personality and a job title. Under that mental model, more agents = more expertise = better. So you install a collection of 154 and feel like you've hired a company.

That model is wrong, and it's why those installs disappoint. A subagent's real product is context isolation, not personality.

Think about what actually happens when you run a test suite, fetch three pages of API docs, or grep a giant monorepo. All that verbose output floods your main conversation, pushing out the stuff you actually care about and degrading the model's focus. Delegate it to a subagent and the mess stays in its context window — you get back a two-line summary. The subagent isn't valuable because it's a "QA expert." It's valuable because it's a firewall that keeps 5,000 tokens of test spew out of your main thread.

Once you internalize that, the failure modes of the 100-agent zoo become obvious:

•
Description collisions. Claude decides which subagent to use by matching your task against each one's description field. Install fifteen agents with fuzzy, overlapping descriptions and the router either picks the wrong one or, worse, picks none and does it inline. Ten sharp descriptions beat a hundred vague ones every time.

•
Context blowback. The very thing that makes subagents great — returning results to your main thread — turns against you at scale. Spawn six parallel research agents that each return a detailed report and you've just dumped six reports into the context you were trying to protect.

•
The persona tax. A 2,000-word "you are a 10x senior staff principal engineer" system prompt is 2,000 tokens the subagent burns before doing anything useful. Sharp beats elaborate.

The teams shipping real value with subagents aren't the ones with the biggest roster. They're the ones who built a small set of context firewalls, each with a razor-sharp description, the minimum tools, and the cheapest model that does the job. The meta-skill is the same one from my Skills and MCP pieces: curate ruthlessly. Subtraction is the whole game.

With that lens, here are the twelve that earn their context.

How I Evaluated 100 Subagents

Each one got scored on five axes:

•
Trigger precision — Does Claude delegate to it at the right moment, and leave it alone otherwise? (This lives or dies on the description.)

•
Context economy — Does it save main-thread context by isolating verbose work, or does it dump a giant report back?

•
Tool hygiene — Minimum necessary permissions. A reviewer has no business holding Write.

•
Model fit — Is it routed to the cheapest model that does the job well?

•
Real weekly fit — Does it map to work I actually do, not a résumé of a job title?

Anything scoring under 3/5 on more than two axes got cut. That eliminated about 80% of what I tried — including almost every hyper-specific "language specialist," which in practice your main model already handles fine.

The 12 Claude Code Subagents Worth Keeping (Ranked)

1. code-reviewer — The one that pays for itself daily

Read-only · Sonnet · runs after every change

The canonical subagent, and the one every serious collection includes for good reason. It runs git diff, focuses on modified files, and returns feedback organized by priority (critical / warnings / suggestions). Crucially it's read-only — Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, no Write or Edit — so it critiques without "helpfully" rewriting your code mid-review. Configure its description with "use immediately after writing or modifying code" and Claude delegates to it proactively, unprompted.

Use it when: After any meaningful change, before you open a PR. This is the subagent I'd install first.

2. debugger — Root cause, not symptom

Read + Edit · inherit · for failures and stack traces

Where the reviewer is read-only, the debugger gets Edit because fixing bugs means changing code. Its prompt encodes a real workflow: capture the error and stack trace, isolate the failure, form and test hypotheses, implement a minimal fix, verify. The value is the discipline — it hunts the underlying cause instead of slapping a patch on the symptom, and it keeps all the noisy log-spelunking in its own context.

Use it when: A test fails, an exception spikes, or behavior goes sideways and you want the why, not just a bandage.

3. test-runner — The purest context firewall

Bash + Read · Haiku or Sonnet · isolates verbose output

This is the subagent that best embodies the whole thesis. Running a test suite produces mountains of output you don't want in your main thread — you want "3 tests failed, here's what and why." Delegate the run to a subagent and exactly that comes back; the 4,000 lines of passing-test spew stay quarantined. It doesn't need a fancy model, so route it to Haiku and it's nearly free. If there's a single subagent that proves subagents aren't about personas, it's this one.

Use it when: Any time you'd otherwise dump a test run, build log, or long command output into your conversation.

4. security-auditor — Deep reasoning, high stakes

Read-only · Opus · for anything that touches trust boundaries

Some work is worth the expensive model. A security audit — authn/authz flows, input validation, secret handling, OWASP-class issues — is exactly where you want deep reasoning, so this one is routed to Opus and kept strictly read-only. It's the clearest example of model routing as judgment: you'd never run every task on Opus, but for the audit that could save you a breach, you absolutely do.

Use it when: Reviewing auth, handling untrusted input, before shipping anything security-sensitive.

5. architect-reviewer — Guardrails for the big decisions

Read-only · Opus · design and structure

A specialized reviewer that evaluates changes against architectural principles — boundaries, coupling, consistency with existing patterns — rather than line-by-line style. Also Opus-routed, because catching a bad structural decision early is worth the tokens. I reach
